Document [2] (Title: Bortezomib -- Contraindications) The drug is not recommended for use in women who are pregnant or breastfeeding. There is a definite risk of harm to the fetus if used during pregnancy. If the female is pregnant and has a life-threatening situation, the use of bortezomib requires a thorough discussion with the patient, oncologists, and members of the ethics committee.
Document [3] (Title: Bortezomib -- Indications -- Non-FDA-Approved Uses) Treatment of antibody-mediated rejection in cardiac transplantation Relapsed or refractory cutaneous T-cell lymphomas Relapsed or refractory follicular lymphoma Relapsed or refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma Systemic light-chain amyloidosis Relapsed or refractory Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia
Document [4] (Title: Bortezomib -- Indications) Bortezomib is FDA-approved for use in the initial treatment of multiple myeloma in combination with cyclophosphamide and dexamethasone. It is also FDA-approved for use in the treatment of multiple myeloma in individuals who previously responded to bortezomib and relapsed at least six months after the completion of the prior treatment. Also, it is FDA-approved for use in the treatment of mantle cell lymphoma in individuals who have received at least one prior first-line treatment. [1] [2] [3]

Document [6] (Title: Bortezomib -- Monitoring -- Special Patient Populations) Elderly: While data are lacking, one should take great precautions when administering bortezomib to seniors because of the risk of toxicity. However, the effect of the drug is the same as in younger patients.
Document [7] (Title: Amyloidosis -- Treatment / Management) High-risk patients (stage IIIb, NYHA class III or above): bortezomib can be preferred due to a rapid onset of action or low-dose combination regimens are preferred as well.
Document [8] (Title: Bortezomib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) Bortezomib is a drug for the treatment of multiple myeloma. Because of its narrow therapeutic index, the drug is used only by a few healthcare professionals. Ordering should be under the direction of an oncology specialist who is well-versed in the use of the drug.  The drug is administered parenterally and requires close monitoring by the nursing staff. Besides checking the CBC, the liver function involves assessment. Before starting the drug, a baseline chest X-ray is recommended.
Document [9] (Title: Bortezomib -- Contraindications) Hypersensitivity reaction to bortezomib or any other components of the formulation like boron, mannitol, etc., is a contraindication. Bortezomib administration should never be via the intrathecal route. [10] [11] [12]

Document [14] (Title: Bortezomib -- Administration) Bortezomib is available in intravenous (IV) and subcutaneous (SQ) forms. There is no difference in the response rates between the available forms; however, the SQ form correlates with a decreased occurrence of grade 3 or higher adverse effects. IV form administration is as a rapid push (3 to 5 seconds). The dosing regimens below will reference IV administration.
Document [15] (Title: Bortezomib -- Administration) For previously relapsing multiple myeloma, bortezomib dosing is 1.3 mg/m^2 X 1 IV on days 1, 4, 8, and 11 of a 21-day cycle. If the patient has previously received bortezomib therapy, give up to 8 cycles, then starting with cycle 9, dosing can be 1.3 mg/m^2 X 1 IV on days 1, 8, 15, and 22 of a 35-day cycle; doses must be at least 72 hours apart. In this regimen, bortezomib can be given with dexamethasone or as monotherapy. [6]
Document [16] (Title: Bortezomib -- Monitoring) Frequently check CBC with differential and platelets during the therapy as it causes myelosuppression and pancytopenia. Cell counts reach a nadir by day 11, and recovery occurs by day 21. Check liver function tests in patients with existing hepatic impairment as it can lead to hepatic failure or abscess formation. Avoid using the drug if liver enzymes increase to 2 to 3 times the upper limit of normal. Evaluate for signs/symptoms of peripheral neuropathy. Frequently assess the volume status and check orthostatic blood pressure during the treatment. Order a baseline chest x-ray and then periodic pulmonary function testing with new or worsening pulmonary symptoms. [13] [14]
Document [17] (Title: Bortezomib -- Monitoring -- Cautions) Peripheral neuropathy: Data show that bortezomib can cause peripheral neuropathy when combined with other medications like isoniazid, amiodarone, and HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors. Thus, close monitoring of neurological deficits is a strong recommendation.
Document [18] (Title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials) The current use of carfilzomib, bortezomib, and ixazomib by targeting the ubiquitin-proteosome pathway has proven an effective treatment strategy in MM patients; however, patients can develop relapse due to drug resistance. Newer agents like proteolysis targeting chimera (PROTAC) are being studied in the latest clinical trials as molecules that target the disease-related protein to induce its proteasomal degradation.
Document [19] (Title: Bortezomib -- Toxicity) Bortezomib should be withheld at the onset of grade 3 non-hematological or grade 4 hematological toxicity until the toxicity resolves. Treatment is then treatment restarted at a lower adjusted dose. There is no antidote available to reverse the toxic effects of the drug.
Document [20] (Title: Bortezomib -- Monitoring -- Special Patient Populations) There have been reports of life-threatening graft versus host disease in patients with myeloma receiving treatment with bortezomib. Thus, all patients with multiple myeloma and lymphoma should receive irradiated blood products to reduce this risk.
Document [21] (Title: Lymphoma -- Treatment / Management -- Mantle Cell Lymphoma) Treatment of the less aggressive disease is with either R- CHOP or R- Benda, followed by maintenance rituximab. Most patients also need more intensive treatment regimens. One agent cytarabine is included in most regimens due to its activity in mantle cell lymphoma. The front line chemotherapy is followed in the first remission by consolidation with high dose chemotherapy and autologous stem cell transplantation. One such approach is the Nordic protocol (a maxi R–CHOP regimen alternating with rituximab and high-dose cytarabine). Another regimen is alternating hyper CVAD (cyclophosphamide, vincristine, doxorubicin, dexamethasone) with methotrexate/cytarabine. Lenalidomide and bortezomib have single-agent activity in relapsed MCL. The Bruton’s tyrosine kinase inhibitor, Ibrutinib, is approved by the FDA for use in relapsed MCL as it has good activity in MCL. [14]
Document [22] (Title: Ixazomib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) Ixazomib is currently the only oral proteasome inhibitor being used in treating multiple myeloma, so all interprofessional healthcare team members, including physicians, specialists, advanced practice practitioners, nursing staff, and pharmacists, should be aware of this drug, its indication, dosing, adverse event profile, potential drug-drug interactions, and requisite monitoring. Due to increasing use and the many adverse effects of ixazomib, the interprofessional team must monitor patients diligently, irrespective of their specific discipline.
Document [23] (Title: Amyloidosis -- Treatment / Management) Intermediate risk patients (ineligible for ASCT and stage I-IIIa): Melphalan and Dexamethasone are the preferred regimens especially in the case of neuropathy or t(11;14) translocation. Cyclophosphamide with Bortezomib and dexamethasone combination is a stem cell sparing regimen preferred in patients with renal failure and with 1q21 gain. If noninvolved Free Light Chains (FLC) is above 180 mg/l, the preferred regimen is bortezomib, melphalan, and dexamethasone combination.
Document [24] (Title: Bortezomib -- Monitoring -- Special Patient Populations) Amyloidosis: Patients with amyloidosis should also receive bortezomib therapy with great caution as there are reports that the use of bortezomib can lead to excess protein accumulation in many organs. Hypotension: Since bortezomib can cause hypotension, dose adjustment of the antihypertensive drugs is required. These patients need close monitoring of their blood pressure.
Document [25] (Title: Bortezomib -- Monitoring -- Special Patient Populations) Diabetes mellitus: Patients taking oral hypoglycemic agents are at risk for developing hypo or hyperglycemia when treated with bortezomib; therefore, the blood sugars require careful monitoring. Most patients do develop hypoglycemia, so reducing the dose of the oral hypoglycemic agent may be necessary.
Document [26] (Title: Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management) The ideal induction regimen is a nuanced decision, and many individual factors must be considered. In general, and based on the Mayo Stratification for Myeloma and Risk-adapted Therapy (mSMART) consensus opinion, for patients who are deemed high-risk disease and are transplant eligible, induction therapy would typically be started with four cycles of daratumumab, bortezomib, lenalidomide, and dexamethasone followed by an early ASCT. Although debatable, long-term survival seems to favor an early transplant approach in high-risk disease. [17] After completion of ASCT, proteasome inhibitor-based maintenance therapy should be initiated and continued until disease progression.
Document [27] (Title: Bortezomib -- Mechanism of Action) Bortezomib is a proteasome inhibitor. The proteasomal system plays a vital role in cellular protein turnover, which is essential for the homeostasis of cells. Bortezomib reversibly binds to the chymotrypsin-like subunit of the 26S proteasome, resulting in its inhibition and preventing the degradation of various pro-apoptotic factors. [4] [5] The accumulation will eventually activate the programmed cell death via caspase-mediated pathways in the neoplastic cells that are usually dependent on the suppression of pro-apoptotic pathways for their proliferation and survival.
Document [28] (Title: Calcium Deposition and Other Renal Crystal Diseases -- Treatment / Management) Standard first-line treatment of newly diagnosed multiple myeloma is the combination of an immunomodulatory drug (lenalidomide), a proteasome inhibitor (bortezomib), and steroids (dexamethasone). [71] Cyclophosphamide or doxorubicin can be used in place of immunomodulatory drugs. [72] Proteasome inhibitors have been shown to improve survival in multiple myeloma across multiple randomized trials and do not require dosage adjustment for renal impairment. Proteasome inhibitors rapidly reduce free light chain concentrations and inhibit the NF-kB inflammatory pathway, thereby decreasing tubular inflammation and apoptosis. Adding monoclonal anti-CD38 antibodies (daratumumab) to the standard of care has improved the complete remission rates in the GRIFFIN and CASSIOPEIA trials. [73] [74] Daratumumab is also an acceptable substitute for patients who cannot tolerate bortezomib for any reason, such as peripheral neuropathy. [75]
Document [29] (Title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Treatment Options) Various anti-myeloma drugs and their combinations are used to select treatment plans for RRMM patients. These include autologous stem cell transplantation, protease inhibitors (PIs), immunomodulatory drugs (IMiDs), targeted agents like monoclonal antibodies (mAbs), antibody-drug conjugates, chimeric antigen receptor T-cells (CAR-T-cells), biphasic T-cell engagers (BiTEs), selinexor, venetoclax, and many others.

Document [31] (Title: Bortezomib -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) Bortezomib therapy requires a collaborative effort from the interprofessional healthcare team. The drug can cause hypotension, and the nurse must check the BP frequently. Nursing staff should report any signs of adverse events to the attending clinician immediately and check with the pharmacist. If there are any questions about the drug, one should consult with the pharmacist first. The pharmacist should also verify all dosing and consult the ordering clinician regarding any discrepancies, as well as performing full medication reconciliation. Before dispensing the drug, the pharmacist should ensure that the patient has no allergies and has been worked up for tuberculosis and perform medication reconciliation. Also, the patient must receive education regarding the potential long-term adverse effects that can occur, including neuropathy.

Document [0] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981) Inhibitory proteas refers to a class of proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Ixazomib is a specific drug that belongs to this inhibitory proteas class, acting as a reversible proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. Remikiren, although primarily recognized as a renin inhibitor, is also categorized within the inhibitory proteas group because it exhibits proteasome‑inhibitory activity, placing it alongside ixazomib as another member of this therapeutic class. This relationship highlights that both ixazomib and remikiren share a common mechanism of proteasome inhibition, despite their differing primary indications.
Document [1] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 679) Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral agents that target viral proteases. Key members of this class include the protease inhibitors ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. Amprenavir is linked to a downstream entity termed inhibitory proteas, reflecting a broader relationship between antiviral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition. Inhibitory proteas is classified as ixazomib, a proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. This connection highlights the pharmacologic overlap where agents originally developed as viral protease inhibitors share mechanistic features with proteasome‑targeting drugs, informing both antiviral therapy and oncology drug development.

In terms of drug‑interaction safety, cisapride monohydrate is contraindicated for use together with amprenavir, as well as with the diuretic furosemide and the antifungal fluconazole. Recognizing these class relationships and contraindications is essential for appropriate prescribing and for avoiding adverse drug interactions.

Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 469) Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a therapeutic class that includes the agents fosamprenavir, amprenavir and tipranavir. These drugs share antiviral activity through inhibition of viral proteases, and the class is linked to a broader pharmacologic relationship with inhibitory proteas, indicating that some members may also affect proteasome function. Amprenavir, in particular, is associated with inhibitory proteas as a downstream entity, suggesting it may possess proteasome‑inhibitory properties in addition to its antiviral effects. The inhibitory proteas group comprises compounds such as hirudiny and ixazomib, both of which are classified as types of proteasome inhibitors. This group is situated within a larger hierarchical category represented by remikiren, which serves as a broader parent class for proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Cisapride monohydrate is identified as contraindicated with several other drugs, specifically furosemid, flukonazol and amprenavir. This contraindication relationship indicates that co‑administration of cisapride monohydrate with any of these agents may lead to adverse effects or reduced therapeutic efficacy. Overall, the knowledge base highlights the interconnected nature of antiviral protease inhibitors, proteasome‑targeting agents, and drug‑interaction warnings, underscoring the importance of recognizing class memberships, hierarchical drug classifications, and contraindication profiles in clinical decision‑making.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2174) Hirudiny is classified as an inhibitory proteas, indicating that it functions as a proteasome‑inhibiting agent. The class of inhibitory proteas is linked to ixazomib, a well‑known proteasome inhibitor drug, and is also related to remikiren, which is identified as a member of this inhibitory proteas category. Together, hirudiny, ixazomib, and remikiren share the common mechanism of proteasome inhibition, a therapeutic strategy employed in conditions such as certain cancers where disruption of protein degradation pathways can be beneficial.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81) Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1221) Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral agents that target viral proteases. Members of this class include ritonavir, fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. Amprenavir is specifically linked to the concept of inhibitory proteas, indicating that it may possess proteasome‑inhibiting activity or be a precursor to such agents. The protease‑inhibitor class as a whole is also described as having a broader relationship with inhibitory proteas, suggesting overlap between viral protease inhibition and proteasome inhibition mechanisms. Compounds that are classified as inhibitory proteas include hirudiny and the approved proteasome inhibitor ixazomib. Remikiren is related to inhibitory proteas in an inverse hierarchical manner, implying that it represents a broader or distinct category that encompasses proteasome‑inhibiting agents. Together, these relationships highlight the interconnected pharmacologic landscape of viral protease inhibitors and proteasome inhibitors, which can be relevant for designing combination therapies and understanding cross‑reactivity in patient care.
